sections.heading: API Reference / class LindbladTTN / Constructor arguments / Method: run(rho0, t_span, dt, observables=None, save_every=1, verbose=True) → LindbladResult / Properties / Example
sections.text: API Reference · LindbladTTN Reference API Reference LindbladTTN exports exactly two public names: the solver class and the result dataclass. Everything else is internal. from lindblad_ttn import LindbladTTN, LindbladResult class LindbladTTN The single user-facing class. Constructing it builds the Liouvillian SoP and the TTN topology; calling .run(...) propagates the density matrix. Constructor arguments Argument Type Default Description H0 np.ndarray | None — Static Hamiltonian, shape $(2^N, 2^N)$. May be None for purely dissipative dynamics. f Callable[[float], float] | None None Drive envelope $f(t)$ for the single-drive shortcut. Pair with V . Mutually exclusive with drives . V np.ndarray | None None Drive operator for the single-drive shortcut. Combined as $H(t) = H_0 + f(t)\,V$. drives list[(Callable, ndarray)] | None None Multi-channel drives: each tuple is $(f_i, V_i)$. The resulting Hamiltonian is $H(t) = H_0 + \sum_i f_i(t)\,V_i$. L_ops list[(float, ndarray)] | None None Lindblad jump operators as (γ, L) tuples. Empty list = unitary evolution. n_sites int 1 Number of qubits. Must be ≥ 1. bond_dim int 32 Maximum TTN bond dimension $\chi$. topology "train" | "tree" "tree" TTN structure. "train" = MPS-style chain; "tree" = balanced binary tree. strategy "ps1" | "vmf" "ps1" TDVP variant. "ps1" = projector-splitting, "vmf" = variable mean-field ODE. device str "cpu" PyTorch device string: "cpu" , "cuda" , "cuda:0" , etc. dtype str | torch.dtype "complex128" Tensor dtype. Use "complex64" on GPU for ~3× speed at ~10⁻⁵ accuracy cost. num_threads int | None None If set, caps PyTorch's intra-op thread count via torch.set_num_threads(...) . parallel_nodes bool False VMF only. Evaluate per-node RHS terms concurrently inside each ODE step. vmf_atol float 1e-8 Absolute tolerance passed to torchdiffeq when strategy="vmf" . ode_method str "dopri5" torchdiffeq integrator name. Any torchdiffeq -supported method is accepted. krylov_dim int 20 Krylov subspace dimension for PS1 local matrix exponentials. units str "natural" Reserved for future use; currently affects no behaviour. Method: run(rho0, t_span, dt, observables=None, save_every=1, verbose=True) → LindbladResult Run the TDVP evolution. Argument Type Default Description rho0 np.ndarray — Initial state. Either a state vector (shape $(2^N,)$, projected to $|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|$ automatically) or a density matrix (shape $(2^N, 2^N)$). Normalised internally to unit trace. t_span tuple[float, float] — Time interval $(t_0, t_f)$. dt float — Fixed step size for PS1, or initial step for the VMF integrator. observables list[np.ndarray] | None None List of $(2^N, 2^N)$ observables. Their expectation values are saved at every save_every steps. save_every int 1 Save the state every $n$ steps. Set higher to reduce memory and I/O. verbose bool True If true, render a tqdm progress bar during propagation. Properties solver.frame — the underlying Frame (TTN topology). Read-only. Example from lindblad_ttn import LindbladTTN import numpy as np sz = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]], dtype=complex) sm = np.array([[0, 0], [1, 0]], dtype=complex) solver = LindbladTTN( H0=0.5 * sz, L_ops=[(0.1, sm)], n_sites=1, bond_dim=4, topology="train", strategy="ps1", ) rho0 = np.array([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.0]], dtype=complex) result = solver.run(rho0=rho0, t_span=(0.0, 10.0), dt=0.01, observables=[sz]) class LindbladResult Dataclass returned by solver.run(...) . Attributes Attribute Type Description times np.ndarray 1D array of saved time points, shape (n_saved,) . expect dict[int, np.ndarray] Maps observable index → complex time series of $\langle O\rangle(t)$, each shape (n_saved,) . rho_final np.ndarray Final density matrix at $t = t_f$, shape $(2^N, 2^N)$. norm np.ndarray $\mathrm{Tr}\,\rho(t)$ at every saved step — should stay at 1 for a trace-preserving evolution. bond_dims np.ndarray Max virtual bond dimension over all node-node edges, at every saved step. Methods Method Description save_txt(path="lindblad_resu

sections.heading: Background / 1. Open quantum systems and the density matrix / 2. The Lindblad master equation / 3. Vectorisation to Liouville space / 4. Pauli decomposition of the Liouvillian / 5. Tree Tensor Networks
sections.text: Background · LindbladTTN Theory Background The physics and the algorithms that LindbladTTN puts together. 1. Open quantum systems and the density matrix A pure quantum state $|\psi\rangle$ is a vector in a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ of dimension $d = 2^N$ for $N$ qubits. When the system is coupled to an environment (a bath, a measurement apparatus, a decoherence channel), the state is no longer pure — it must be described by a density matrix : $$\rho = \sum_k p_k\,|\psi_k\rangle\langle\psi_k|, \qquad \rho \in \mathbb{C}^{d\times d}, \quad \rho = \rho^\dagger, \quad \mathrm{Tr}\,\rho = 1, \quad \rho \ge 0.$$ Expectation values of observables are computed as $\langle O \rangle = \mathrm{Tr}(O\rho)$. 2. The Lindblad master equation Under the Born–Markov and rotating-wave approximations, the dynamics of $\rho$ are governed by the Gorini–Kossakowski–Sudarshan–Lindblad (GKSL) master equation: $$ \frac{d\rho}{dt} = -\frac{i}{\hbar}\,[H(t),\,\rho] + \sum_{k} \gamma_k\!\left(L_k\,\rho\,L_k^\dagger - \tfrac{1}{2}\{L_k^\dagger L_k,\,\rho\}\right). $$ The first term is unitary evolution under the Hamiltonian $H(t)$. The sum encodes dissipation: each jump operator $L_k$ with rate $\gamma_k$ represents an irreversible channel (e.g. $L = \sigma_-$ for spontaneous emission, $L = \sigma_z$ for pure dephasing). The GKSL form is the most general Markovian generator that preserves trace, hermiticity, and positivity of $\rho$ — i.e. it preserves physicality. 3. Vectorisation to Liouville space Working with a matrix equation is awkward. By stacking the columns of $\rho$ into a vector $|\rho\rangle\!\rangle$, the master equation becomes a linear ODE: $$\frac{d}{dt}\,|\rho\rangle\!\rangle = \mathcal{L}(t)\,|\rho\rangle\!\rangle$$ where $\mathcal{L}$ is the Liouvillian superoperator , a matrix of size $d^2 \times d^2$. The commutator and the dissipator both lift to $\mathcal{L}$ via $$ -i[H,\rho]\;\longleftrightarrow\; -i\,(H\otimes I - I\otimes H^T)\,|\rho\rangle\!\rangle, \qquad L\rho L^\dagger \;\longleftrightarrow\; (L\otimes L^*)\,|\rho\rangle\!\rangle. $$ LindbladTTN uses an interleaved per-qubit vectorisation: indices for qubit $s$ are $(i_s, j_s)$ for ket/bra, kept adjacent. This makes per-site operations clean and SoP-friendly. 4. Pauli decomposition of the Liouvillian Every $d\times d$ operator on $N$ qubits expands in the Pauli basis. LindbladTTN decomposes $H$ and each $L_k$ this way, then lifts each Pauli string into the Liouville superoperator: $$\mathcal{L} = \sum_k c_k \bigotimes_{s=1}^{N} O_s^{(k)}, \qquad O_s^{(k)} \in \mathbb{C}^{4\times 4}.$$ This is a Sum-of-Products (SoP) representation: a list of terms, each a tensor product of local 4×4 superoperators (one per qubit). It avoids ever materialising the full $4^N \times 4^N$ Liouvillian. 5. Tree Tensor Networks A Tree Tensor Network represents the vectorised density matrix $|\rho\rangle\!\rangle \in \mathbb{C}^{4^N}$ as a tree of low-rank tensors: $$|\rho\rangle\!\rangle = \mathrm{Tr}_{\text{bonds}} \prod_{\text{nodes}} T_n,$$ where each node tensor $T_n$ has axes for its physical degrees of freedom (some 4-dimensional Liouville indices) and for its bonds to other nodes. The maximum size of a bond is the bond dimension $\chi$. Two topologies are supported: Train (MPS-like) — qubits arranged in a chain. Optimal for nearest-neighbour interactions in 1D. Balanced binary tree — log-depth tree of nodes. Reduces the diameter of the network, better for problems with many long-range couplings. Crucially, the cost of operations on a TTN scales polynomially in $\chi$, not exponentially in $N$. The bond dimension acts as a single knob between cost and accuracy. 6. TDVP: Time-Dependent Variational Principle To evolve a TTN we project the exact derivative $\mathcal{L}|\rho\rangle\!\rangle$ onto the tangent space of the manifold of rank-$\chi$ TTNs at the current point. This is the Dirac–Frenkel TDVP : $$\frac{d|\rho\rangle\!\rangle}{dt} = P_{T_{\rho}\mathcal{M}}\,\mathcal{L}\,|\rho\rangle\!\r

sections.heading: How to cite / Software citation / BibTeX / APA / Background references
sections.text: Cite · LindbladTTN Citation How to cite If LindbladTTN supports work in a publication, please cite the repository. A companion paper is forthcoming. Software citation Until the paper is out, cite the GitHub repository directly. A placeholder BibTeX entry is below — update the URL and commit hash to match the version you used. BibTeX @software{lindblad_ttn_2026, author = {LindbladTTN contributors}, title = {LindbladTTN: Tree Tensor Network Lindblad Master Equation Solver}, year = {2026}, publisher = {GitHub}, url = {https://github.com/<your-org>/lindblad_ttn}, version = {0.2.0}, note = {Open-source Python library} } APA LindbladTTN contributors. (2026). LindbladTTN: Tree Tensor Network Lindblad master equation solver (Version 0.2.0) [Computer software]. https://github.com/<your-org>/lindblad_ttn Background references If you cite LindbladTTN, please also reference the foundational papers on Tree Tensor Networks and the TDVP, along with the related TENSO project that inspired LindbladTTN's architecture. Schollwöck, U. The density-matrix renormalization group in the age of matrix product states. Annals of Physics 326 , 96–192 (2011). Haegeman, J., Lubich, C., Oseledets, I., Vandereycken, B. & Verstraete, F. Unifying time evolution and optimization with matrix product states. Phys. Rev. B 94 , 165116 (2016). Chen, X. & Franco, I. Tree tensor network hierarchical equations of motion based on time-dependent variational principle for efficient open quantum dynamics in structured thermal environments. J. Chem. Phys. 163 , 104109 (2025). DOI A note on TENSO LindbladTTN is architecturally inspired by pytenso but written entirely from scratch and specialised for the Lindblad master equation. If you use both in a study, cite both.

sections.heading: Get in touch / Issues & bug reports / Contributing / Authors / Related projects
sections.text: Contact · LindbladTTN Contact Get in touch Bug reports, feature requests, scientific questions, and pull requests are all welcome. Issues & bug reports The fastest path is the GitHub issue tracker. Please include: A minimal reproducing example (smallest n_sites / bond_dim / time span that triggers the issue). The full traceback (or a description of the wrong behaviour). Your Python, PyTorch, and CUDA versions. Whether you're on CPU or GPU. Open an issue Contributing LindbladTTN follows a few simple conventions: Tests first. New physics or algorithms should add a QuTiP cross-check to tests/ . Docstrings in NumPy style. Public functions/classes get a one-line summary, a Parameters block, and a Returns block. One feature per PR. Keeps reviews fast and bisects easy. For a discussion before opening a PR (especially for larger changes), file an issue first. Authors L LindbladTTN contributors Maintainers — open source, accepting PRs Related projects TENSO — TTN-HEOM for structured bosonic environments. LindbladTTN borrows its architectural blueprint. QuTiP — the reference open-system solver. LindbladTTN is validated against qutip.mesolve in every example. PyTorch — the tensor backend. torchdiffeq — the ODE integrator powering the VMF strategy.

sections.heading: Examples / Single-qubit decay / Driven qubit (Rabi + decay) / Bell-state generation / Five-qubit cat state / Cascade with Gaussian drives
sections.text: Examples · LindbladTTN Gallery Examples Worked notebooks covering single-qubit, driven, multi-qubit, and benchmark scenarios. Each one is a standalone Jupyter notebook and an executable Python script in the repository. Single-qubit decay Spontaneous emission of a two-level atom. The smallest non-trivial example — validates LindbladTTN against qutip.mesolve to ~10⁻⁷. 1 qubit PS1 qutip Driven qubit (Rabi + decay) A coherent drive $\Omega\cos(\omega_d t)\,\sigma_x$ plus T1 decay. Rabi oscillations damp into the steady state. Demonstrates the f, V single-drive shortcut. 1 qubit time-dep PS1 Bell-state generation Rx(π/2) on qubit 0 followed by a cross-resonance ZX gate, with realistic T1/T2 decoherence. Tracks concurrence and fidelity to the ideal Bell state. 2 qubits gates T1/T2 Five-qubit cat state Prepare and propagate a GHZ-style cat state $(|00000\rangle + |11111\rangle)/\sqrt{2}$ with single-qubit decay on each site. Compares train vs tree topology. 5 qubits cat state tree Cascade with Gaussian drives Five qubits driven by a cascade of Gaussian pulses on different sites — uses the drives=[(f_i, V_i), …] multi-channel API. 5 qubits multi-drive pulses Scaling benchmark Wall-clock time and memory vs system size for $N = 2, 4, 6, 8$ qubits at fixed bond dimension. Includes a CPU↔GPU comparison. benchmark GPU Running the examples locally Each notebook in the gallery corresponds to a script in the examples/ folder. Clone the repository, install LindbladTTN (and the example extras), then run either form: pip install -e . pip install matplotlib qutip jupyter # Run the script directly python examples/qutip_simple.py # Or open the notebook jupyter notebook docs/notebooks/single_qubit_decay.ipynb The example scripts save figures next to themselves; the notebooks bake the plots directly into their outputs.

sections.heading: Getting Started / 1. Define the system / 2. Build the solver / 3. Run the evolution / 4. Inspect results / Next steps
sections.text: Getting Started · LindbladTTN Tutorial Getting Started A complete LindbladTTN workflow in four steps, using single-qubit spontaneous emission — the smallest physically meaningful problem. The problem: a two-level system with frequency $\omega$ undergoing T1 decay at rate $\gamma$. The state evolves according to the Lindblad master equation : $$ \frac{d\rho}{dt} = -i\,[H,\rho] + \gamma\left(\sigma_-\,\rho\,\sigma_+ - \tfrac{1}{2}\{\sigma_+\sigma_-,\,\rho\}\right), \qquad H = \tfrac{\omega}{2}\sigma_z. $$ Starting from the excited state $\rho_0 = |1\rangle\langle 1|$, the population $\langle\sigma_z\rangle$ should decay exponentially from $-1$ to $+1$ (ground state). 1. Define the system Build the Pauli operators, the Hamiltonian $H_0$, and the list of Lindblad jump operators. Each jump operator is a tuple (γ, L) : the rate and the operator. import numpy as np from lindblad_ttn import LindbladTTN # Pauli operators sx = np.array([[0, 1], [1, 0]], dtype=complex) sy = np.array([[0, -1j], [1j, 0]], dtype=complex) sz = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]], dtype=complex) sm = np.array([[0, 0], [1, 0]], dtype=complex) # |0> 2. Build the solver Instantiate LindbladTTN . The four most important arguments are n_sites , bond_dim , topology , and strategy . solver = LindbladTTN( H0=H0, f=None, V=None, # no time-dependent drive L_ops=L_ops, n_sites=1, # one qubit bond_dim=4, # max TTN rank topology="train", # or "tree" for balanced binary tree strategy="ps1", # or "vmf" for adaptive ODE integration device="cpu", # or "cuda" dtype="complex128", ) For a single qubit the bond dimension is over-specified — the state lives in a 4-dimensional Liouville space and even bond_dim=4 is exact. For larger systems, the bond dimension is the key knob between cost and accuracy. Train or tree? Use topology="train" for chains and 1D problems, "tree" when every qubit interacts with many others. The two topologies hold the same information; their bond-dimension scaling differs by problem structure. 3. Run the evolution Call solver.run(...) with an initial density matrix and the time grid. Observables are tracked over time and returned as numpy arrays. rho0 = np.array([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.0]], dtype=complex) # |1> The expectation value of an observable $O$ is computed at every saved step as $\langle O\rangle(t) = \mathrm{Tr}\!\left[O\,\rho(t)\right]$. 4. Inspect results The returned LindbladResult dataclass holds everything the solver saved during the run. import matplotlib.pyplot as plt print("Final trace:", result.norm[-1]) # ~1.0 (trace-preserving) print("Max bond dim:", result.bond_dims.max()) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4)) ax.plot(result.times, result.expect[0].real, label=r"$\langle\sigma_z\rangle$") ax.plot(result.times, result.expect[1].real, label=r"$\langle\sigma_x\rangle$") ax.plot(result.times, result.expect[2].real, label=r"$\langle\sigma_y\rangle$") ax.set_xlabel("t"); ax.set_ylabel("expectation"); ax.legend() plt.show() # Save a full plain-text report result.save_txt("single_qubit_decay.txt") You should see $\langle\sigma_z\rangle$ rise from $-1$ to $+1$ (ground state population) with the time constant $1/\gamma$, while $\langle\sigma_x\rangle$ and $\langle\sigma_y\rangle$ oscillate at $\omega$ and decay at $\gamma/2$. That's the spontaneous emission picture exactly. Next steps Examples — Bell-state generation, driven qubits, multi-qubit cat states, scaling benchmarks. Background — the math behind the master equation, TTN, and TDVP. API reference — every constructor argument and result field. Input generator — autogenerate a starting script for your own problem.

title: LindbladTTN — Tree Tensor Network Lindblad Solver

sections.heading: LindbladTTN / A scalable open-system solver / TTN Compression / PS1 & VMF / Tree & Train / Time-Dependent Drives
sections.text: LindbladTTN — Tree Tensor Network Lindblad Solver Open Quantum Dynamics · TTN-TDVP LindbladTTN Tree Tensor Network Lindblad Master Equation Solver Lindblad master equation, solved. Get Started GitHub Polynomial Memory via TTN PS1 + VMF TDVP Strategies CPU · GPU Via PyTorch |q₀⟩ |q₁⟩ |q₂⟩ |q₃⟩ Why LindbladTTN A scalable open-system solver LindbladTTN exposes a single class. Inside, it builds the Liouvillian as a Sum-of-Products operator, decomposes the density matrix as a Tree Tensor Network, and evolves it with a TDVP integrator — all on top of PyTorch. TTN Compression The density matrix lives in a tree of low-rank tensors. Bond dimension controls the accuracy/cost tradeoff — pick the rank that fits your problem. PS1 & VMF Two complementary TDVP strategies: projector-splitting (PS1) for fixed-rank stability, and Variable Mean-Field (VMF) for adaptive ODE integration via torchdiffeq . Tree & Train Choose topology="train" for an MPS-style chain or "tree" for a balanced binary tree. The frame factory wires up either topology automatically. Time-Dependent Drives Pass f(t) and V for a single drive, or a list of (f_i, V_i) tuples for multi-pulse circuits — every channel becomes a SoP term. GPU via PyTorch The same code runs on CPU or CUDA — set device="cuda" . On GPU, drop to dtype="complex64" for a ~3× speedup at ~10⁻⁵ accuracy cost. QuTiP-Validated Every example ships with a side-by-side QuTiP comparison. Single-qubit, driven, and multi-qubit dynamics all agree with qutip.mesolve to ~10⁻⁷. Quick Start A simulation in four steps Define the system, build the solver, propagate, inspect. 1. System 2. Solver 3. Run 4. Inspect import numpy as np from lindblad_ttn import LindbladTTN # Pauli operators sz = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]], dtype=complex) sm = np.array([[0, 0], [1, 0]], dtype=complex) # sigma minus # Single-qubit decay: H = omega/2 sz, jump = sqrt(gamma) sigma- omega, gamma = 1.0, 0.3 H0 = 0.5 * omega * sz L_ops = [(gamma, sm)] solver = LindbladTTN( H0=H0, f=None, V=None, # no drive in this example L_ops=L_ops, n_sites=1, bond_dim=4, topology="train", strategy="ps1", device="cpu", dtype="complex128", ) # Initial state: excited |1> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt print("Final trace:", result.norm[-1]) print("Final density matrix:\n", result.rho_final) plt.plot(result.times, result.expect[0].real, label=r"$\langle\sigma_z\rangle$") plt.xlabel("t"); plt.ylabel("expectation") plt.legend(); plt.show() # Save full report to plain text result.save_txt("decay.txt") Full Tutorial Architecture Three layers, one class Everything users touch is LindbladTTN . Underneath, three independent layers do the work. core/ TTN infrastructure: backend, graph topology (Node/End/Frame), per-node tensor storage, gauge transformations, Sum-of-Products operators. physics/ The Lindblad layer: Pauli decomposition of the Liouvillian into local 4×4 superoperators, Liouville-space vec/unvec, train and balanced-tree frame factories. propagation/ TDVP engine: PS1 projector-splitting sweeps, VMF whole-tensor ODE integration, Krylov matrix exponentials, local effective Liouvillians. Full architecture

sections.heading: Input Generator
sections.text: Input Generator · LindbladTTN Tool Input Generator Pick the knobs on the left — a ready-to-run LindbladTTN script appears on the right. The output updates as you type. System Number of qubits Qubit frequency ω Decay rate γ Drive (optional) Add a coherent drive on qubit 0 Rabi amplitude Ω_R TTN Bond dimension χ Topology tree train Propagation Strategy ps1 vmf Device cpu cuda dtype complex128 complex64 Final time Step size dt Copy code What this builds The generator emits a self-contained Python script that defines Pauli operators, embeds them on each qubit, builds the Hamiltonian and a local-decay channel on every site, instantiates LindbladTTN , and runs a single TDVP trajectory from $\rho_0 = |0\ldots 0\rangle\langle 0\ldots 0|$. Edit it further to match your specific physics.

sections.heading: Installation / Requirements / Install from source / GPU setup / Running the examples / Running the tests
sections.text: Installation · LindbladTTN Setup Installation LindbladTTN is a pure-Python package built on top of PyTorch. The same install works on CPU and on CUDA-enabled GPUs. Requirements Python ≥ 3.11 PyTorch ≥ 2.0 — tensor operations, autograd, GPU support torchdiffeq ≥ 0.2 — adaptive ODE integration (used by the VMF strategy) numpy / scipy — operator construction, input handling tqdm — progress bars during propagation Optional for the examples and notebooks: matplotlib ≥ 3.7 — plotting qutip ≥ 5.0 — side-by-side validation against the standard open-system solver Install from source Clone the repository and install in editable mode: git clone https://github.com/<your-org>/lindblad_ttn.git cd lindblad_ttn pip install -e . The editable install ( -e . ) lets you modify the source and have the changes picked up immediately without reinstalling. Why source-only? LindbladTTN is still pre-1.0. A PyPI release will follow once the API stabilises and the accompanying paper is out. GPU setup If you have a CUDA-capable GPU, install the matching PyTorch wheel before installing LindbladTTN. Pick the right command from pytorch.org for your CUDA version. For example, CUDA 12.1: pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121 pip install -e . Then pass device="cuda" when constructing the solver: solver = LindbladTTN( H0=H0, L_ops=L_ops, n_sites=8, bond_dim=64, device="cuda", dtype="complex64", # 3x faster on GPU than complex128 ) complex64 trades ~10⁻⁵ accuracy for ~3× speedup. Stay with complex128 on CPU. Running the examples Install the optional plotting and validation packages, then run any script in examples/ : pip install matplotlib qutip python examples/qutip_simple.py Each example prints a max-error metric against qutip.mesolve and saves a PNG comparison plot next to the script. Running the tests pip install pytest pytest tests/ The test suite validates LindbladTTN's outputs against QuTiP for the single-qubit, driven, and small multi-qubit cases. Next steps You're set up — head to the Getting Started tutorial for a four-step walk-through, or jump straight to the Examples gallery .

sections.heading: Code Structure / Project layout / Layer 1 — core/ / core/backend.py / core/graph.py / core/model.py
sections.text: Structure · LindbladTTN Architecture Code Structure LindbladTTN is organised in three independent layers under a single user-facing class. Project layout lindblad_ttn/ ├── __init__.py # Public exports: LindbladTTN, LindbladResult ├── solver.py # Main entry point — the only class users touch ├── time_dependent.py # H(t) = H0 + Σᵢ fᵢ(t)·Vᵢ composition ├── core/ # ← Layer 1: TTN infrastructure │ ├── backend.py │ ├── graph.py │ ├── model.py │ ├── sop.py │ └── gauge.py ├── physics/ # ← Layer 2: Lindblad master equation │ ├── liouvillian.py │ ├── liouville.py │ └── frame_factory.py └── propagation/ # ← Layer 3: TDVP time evolution ├── tdvp.py ├── effective_h.py └── integrators.py Layer 1 — core/ The TTN infrastructure. Everything in core/ is physics-agnostic — it knows about tensors, bonds, and tree topologies, not about quantum operators. core/backend.py PyTorch backend: device and dtype globals ( DEVICE , DTYPE ), set_device(...) , set_dtype(...) , truncating SVD helper, Krylov matrix-exponential. core/graph.py Tree topology primitives: Point , Node , End , Frame . Tracks node adjacency, axis duality, and provides BFS/DFS visitors for traversals. core/model.py The Model class stores one PyTorch tensor per node and tracks the current gauge centre. core/sop.py SumOfProducts — canonical form Σ_k c_k ⊗_s O_s^(k). Each term is a product of local matrices keyed by DOF index. Supports batched contraction via opt_multitransform . core/gauge.py QR-based gauge moves that shift the gauge centre between neighbouring nodes during PS1 sweeps. Layer 2 — physics/ The Lindblad-equation layer. Translates Hamiltonians and jump operators into a SoP Liouvillian, and wires up TTN topologies appropriate for an open quantum system. physics/liouvillian.py LiouvillianSoP — builds the Liouvillian from a Hamiltonian and a list of jump operators. pauli_decompose(...) expands every operator in the Pauli basis and lifts the result to local 4×4 superoperators. physics/liouville.py Liouville-space utilities: vec(ρ) / unvec(v) , trace_from_vec(...) , expect_from_vec(...) . Used for I/O at the boundaries of the TTN propagation. physics/frame_factory.py LindbladFrameFactory.train() and .balanced_tree() build the appropriate TTN topology and the DOF-to-End mapping for the given number of qubits. Layer 3 — propagation/ The TDVP engine. Drives the TTN forward in time, given a SoP Liouvillian. propagation/tdvp.py TDVPPropagator — the workhorse class. Implements both strategies: PS1 (one-site projector-splitting sweeps with QR gauge transformations) and VMF (whole-network ODE integration via torchdiffeq ). propagation/effective_h.py SimpleEffectiveH — bottom-up contraction of all child sub-trees into a local effective Liouvillian acting on a single node. Used at every step of a PS1 sweep. propagation/integrators.py krylov_expm_apply(...) — Krylov-subspace approximation of $\exp(-i\,\mathcal{L}_{\text{eff}}\,t)\,|\psi\rangle$ for the on-node and on-bond evolutions inside PS1. User-facing entry point Everything above is wrapped in one class: solver.py LindbladTTN is the only public class. Its constructor accepts $H_0$, drives, jump operators, and TTN/TDVP options; .run() propagates and returns a LindbladResult . See the API reference for every argument. The companion time_dependent.py module composes static and time-dependent SoP terms into the time-evaluated Liouvillian at each step — supports both the single-drive shortcut ( f, V ) and arbitrary drives=[(f_i, V_i), …] lists. Data flow at a glance Build — User passes Hamiltonian, jumps, topology, bond_dim to LindbladTTN(...) . physics turns operators into a SoP Liouvillian; physics.frame_factory builds a TTN topology; core represents it. Initial state — solver.run(rho0=…) compresses the vectorised $|\rho_0\rangle\!\rangle$ into the TTN via sequential SVD (an MPS-style sweep). Propagate — At every step, propagation.tdvp applies the chosen TDVP strategy. For time-dependent problems, time_dependent rebuilds the SoP at the st
